critique.summary: The article explains the concept of a "molly guard," a protective cover or mechanism designed to prevent accidental pressing of important buttons, often named after an engineer's daughter who accidentally pressed a critical button. It discusses examples of molly guards in hardware and software, and introduces the idea of "reverse molly guards," which are buttons that activate automatically after inactivity.
mainPoints: A molly guard is a plastic safety cover or mechanism that prevents accidental pressing of significant buttons.
The term allegedly originates from an engineer's daughter named Molly who accidentally pressed a big red button in a datacenter.
Molly guards appear in various forms in hardware, such as recessed buttons, plastic ridges, and SIM card ejection holes.
In software, molly guards manifest as confirmation dialogs or requiring multiple key presses (e.g., Ctrl+Alt+Del).
Reverse molly guards are buttons that activate themselves after a period of inactivity, which can be helpful in some programming contexts.
critique.truthfulness: The concept of a molly guard as a protective cover for buttons is well-known in engineering and safety design. The anecdote about the engineer's daughter named Molly is a commonly cited but unverified origin story, making it more of a folklore than a confirmed fact. The examples of molly guards in hardware and software are accurate and consistent with known safety mechanisms. The idea of reverse molly guards is less commonly discussed but plausible as a design pattern. Overall, the article's claims are mostly accurate, though the origin story should be treated as anecdotal rather than factual.
considerations: The article does not provide verifiable sources or multiple perspectives to confirm the origin story of the term 'molly guard.'
It does not discuss any potential drawbacks or criticisms of molly guards, such as user inconvenience or failure modes.
The article lacks technical details or references to standards or best practices related to molly guards in engineering.
The concept of reverse molly guards could be expanded with examples or discussion of contexts where they are beneficial or problematic.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around the software tool 'molly-guard', which prevents accidental shutdowns of remote servers by requiring confirmation. Commenters expand on the concept by relating it to defensive design principles like Poka-Yoke, debating its applicability in software, and sharing related anecdotes.

comments.author: JoshTriplett
comments.text: There's a great piece of software called "molly-guard", which intercepts calls to "poweroff" and "reboot" and similar. It checks if it's being invoked via an SSH session, and if so, it asks you to type the name of the system you're shutting down. That way, you never accidentally shut down a remote server when you meant to shut down your own system (or a different server).
comments.analysis: This comment accurately describes the functionality and purpose of molly-guard, providing a clear and informative explanation.

comments.author: 0xbadcafebee
comments.text:
In DevOps (and Lean, TPS) the more advanced form of this is the Poka-Yoke (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Poka-yoke). Poka-yokes don't just add safety, they also guide the human away from making a mistake.

The canonical example is the automatic shift knob in a car. The shift knob is designed to 1) prevent you from accidentally shifting all the way back into reverse without pressing the shift button, and 2) prevents you from leaving park or neutral without depressing the brake pedal. This way you don't damage the drivetrain or accidentally cause the car to roll forward/backward.

Poka-yoke is a form of defensive design (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Defensive_design). For a beautiful example of defensive design, see the average electric kettle. If water boils over the top it won't short the device, if it boils dry it'll stop operating, the handle and body are plastic to prevent burning yourself, the handle is ergonomic to make carrying 1.5L of sloshing boiling water not cause you to spill it, the cord is detached from the kettle so you don't yank the cord and spill the boiling water, the switches are located on the bottom away from hot steam, and the lids usually lock while in operation, again to prevent damage from spillage or steam. It's the simplest and safest possible way to boil water, and it's $20.
comments.analysis: The comment thoughtfully connects molly-guard to established defensive design concepts, providing relevant examples to illustrate the principle.

comments.author: davidshepherd7
comments.text: That page is copied verbatim from https://unsung.aresluna.org/molly-guard-in-reverse/ (which is linked at the top). The original page also has much better formatting.
comments.analysis: This comment provides useful context about the source of the content, contributing to understanding the origin and quality of the information.

comments.author: fainpul
comments.text: Just please don't start adding molly-guards to your software. The concept only makes sense in the physical world, e.g. where the "important button", that you might never have to press, needs to be in reach all the time. In software, there are better solutions.
comments.analysis: The comment raises a valid point about the limitations of molly-guard style protections in software, suggesting alternative solutions might be better.

comments.author: jiehong
comments.text:
Oh! Then perhaps the long press required for the iPhone’s action button to trigger is a Molly guard!

Also, perhaps `rm` should be molly guarded to move things to the trash on all systems by default, and delete only if forced to by a flag.

Note: I’d have expected Molly to be a cat, because they tend to be pretty good at disrupting things in my experience.
comments.analysis: This comment offers creative analogies and suggestions for extending the molly-guard concept to other interfaces, showing thoughtful engagement.

comments.author: buf
comments.text: Fun random fact, Eventbrite was first a security company called Molly Guard. I spent years cleaning out the 'mg-' prefixes from the code.
comments.analysis: Provides an interesting anecdote related to the name Molly Guard, adding historical context though less relevant to the main technical discussion.

comments.author: donut
comments.text: Sometimes a pop-up appears that I blindly accept because I happen to be typing something with spaces. Wish that button was protected somehow.
comments.analysis: Expresses a common user frustration that supports the need for protective measures, though the comment is more anecdotal than technical.

comments.author: evanjrowley
comments.text: I'm reminded of this legendary HN comment: https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=16530398
comments.analysis: This comment provides a link without context or explanation, limiting its usefulness in the discussion.

comments.author: hyperhello
comments.text: “Mollyguarding” sounds like a great derogation of unnecessary safety measures. Stop mollyguarding me!
comments.analysis: This comment is mostly emotional and dismissive, adding little constructive value to the conversation.

comments.author: jiehong
comments.text: I do wish those were a thing on flat touch sensitive induction cooktops! (For all those pesky water droplets causing the cooktop to error out and turning itself off)
comments.analysis: Suggests a practical application of molly-guard style protections in a different context, showing thoughtful consideration.

comments.author: Shadowmist
comments.analysis: Expresses enthusiasm but adds no informational content or analysis.
